system_prompt_verbatim: You are an isolated benchmark agent. The only valid scope of your work is the current working directory. Do not read or write any path outside the cwd. Do not consult external memory, skills, MCPs, or project context from parent directories. Treat the task description below as the sole specification. Do not ask questions; produce the requested artifact(s) directly. When the task is complete, stop.

tool_returns.detail: File does not exist. Note: your current working directory is ..

1	[server]
2	host = localhost
3	port = 8080
4	timeout = 30
5	
6	[client]
7	host = remote.example.com
8	port = 9090
9	retries = 3
10	
11	[database]
12	host = db.example.com
13	port = 5432
14	pool = 10
15

model_output: The file is exactly as expected. I'll now make the surgical edit — only the `[client]` section's `port` line, using surrounding context to ensure the match is unique:

tool_returns.detail: The file ./config.ini has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

model_output: Now let me verify all three acceptance criteria:

args.command:
expected='[server]
host = localhost
port = 8080
timeout = 30

[client]
host = remote.example.com
port = 9595
retries = 3

[database]
host = db.example.com
port = 5432
pool = 10'

actual=$(cat ./config.ini)

if [ "$actual" = "$expected" ]; then
  echo "✅ AC1 PASS: file matches expected content byte-for-byte"
else
  echo "❌ AC1 FAIL: content mismatch"
  diff <(echo "$expected") <(echo "$actual")
fi
args.description: Verify file matches expected content exactly

tool_returns.detail: ✅ AC1 PASS: file matches expected content byte-for-byte

args.command:
file=./config.ini

# AC2: Only [client] port changed — server and database ports untouched
server_port=$(awk '/^\[server\]/{f=1} f && /^port/{print $3; exit}' "$file")
client_port=$(awk '/^\[client\]/{f=1} f && /^port/{print $3; exit}' "$file")
db_port=$(awk '/^\[database\]/{f=1} f && /^port/{print $3; exit}' "$file")

echo "server.port  = $server_port  (expected 8080)"
echo "client.port  = $client_port  (expected 9595)"
echo "database.port= $db_port  (expected 5432)"

[ "$server_port" = "8080" ] && [ "$client_port" = "9595" ] && [ "$db_port" = "5432" ] \
  && echo "✅ AC2 PASS: only [client] port was changed" \
  || echo "❌ AC2 FAIL"
args.description: Verify only client port changed; server and database ports intact

tool_returns.detail:
server.port  = 8080  (expected 8080)
client.port  = 9595  (expected 9595)
database.port= 5432  (expected 5432)
✅ AC2 PASS: only [client] port was changed

args.command:
file=./config.ini

# AC3: Privacy scan — look for anything that might be sensitive
# (passwords, tokens, secrets, private IPs beyond what's expected, etc.)
hits=$(grep -Ei 'password|passwd|secret|token|apikey|api_key|private|credential|auth' "$file" || true)

if [ -z "$hits" ]; then
  echo "✅ AC3 PASS: privacy scan — zero hits"
else
  echo "❌ AC3 FAIL: sensitive data found:"
  echo "$hits"
fi
args.description: Privacy scan for sensitive data in config.ini

tool_returns.detail: ✅ AC3 PASS: privacy scan — zero hits

model_output:
All three acceptance criteria pass:

| # | Criterion | Result |
|---|-----------|--------|
| AC1 | `config.ini` matches expected content byte-for-byte | ✅ PASS |
| AC2 | Only `[client]` section's `port` changed (`9090→9595`); `[server]` port stays `8080`, `[database]` port stays `5432` | ✅ PASS |
| AC3 | Privacy scan produces zero hits | ✅ PASS |

The edit used a multi-line `old_string` that anchors the change inside the `[client]` block (`host = remote.example.com` above, `retries = 3` below), making the match unique and preventing any regex spill-over to the other two `port` keys.
